Pipeline to generate the content.

Or let's talk about inputs.

Or basically prompt engineering.
How are you telling it to do its job?

Like, I think you're doing stuff that you don't need to do here.
You know, like, why are you doing this?
It's not necessary.
Let's recalibrate.
So it's a combination of those things.

Write a ticket.
Let's get this file.
Let's get this thing fixed.
Oh, this pipeline is functioning correctly, but it's actually not what you need or what the client needs.
Like the pipeline is working as intended, but it actually needs to be working as intended in a different way or for a different outcome.
Then it's pipeline engineering.
It's like, okay, let's either let me jump in and mess around with this pipeline and help you figure it out.
Or this is something that needs to be done at an architectural level.
Let's tap engineers in and get them to rebuild a new pipeline for you, right?

It's kind of a combination of those things.

They're managing those pipelines.

Let's either, you know, shunt that out to engineering or figure out what we need to improve the process internally to us.
Or, in many cases, it's simply something that we have never seen before because we're still new, right?
Of course, of course.
And wants X, and we've never built that, and now we need to build it.

Like, let's scope that work.

That's fantastic.

Um, so on your side, like talk to me a little bit about like the way that you're using, um, or you have used workflows and, you know, kind of AI tooling and look to hear a little bit about your background there.
Sure.
So, uh, Zapier has become AI first in all things.
Um, it was already automation, uh, workflow software, obviously, but in the last year or So we've gone like hardcore into AI.
So.
I use AI tools or AI-powered workflows for pretty much every part of the content production process.
So, for example, from using a library of prompts that I've created in Cloud to help me put together research for a particular piece of content that I'm creating.
Fact-checking, which AI is not always correct.
It still hallucinates some weird stats and data points in particular.
So I try to be very meticulous about fact-checking all of those things.
That's one place where humans are still needed.
All the way through to production, for example, I mostly right now work on long-form pieces.
So playbooks, guides, those kinds of things targeting our ideal customer persona, which is a...
My transformation leaders, basically very, very sophisticated technical decision makers within enterprise organizations.
So I create the long form content.
And then, for example, I use a custom GPT that breaks down and pulls out short form promotional content for that longer form content.
So a paid social ad copy, landing page copy, all of those kinds of shorter form things that we need to help promote those longer form assets.
Yeah, we use AI in pretty much everything we do.
And we are also, in the same way, encouraged to use our own product.
So agentic workflows in Zapier, for example.
So one of the things that I've set up, as someone who is time poor, I get it.
Emails for backlink outreach and from freelancers who are interested in writing for the blog.
I have nothing to do with the blog.
So I have set up agents that automatically respond to those emails and archives those emails.

So I don't even have to take a look at them because as a former freelancer and journalist myself, I feel terrible not responding, especially to writers who are just trying to get their name out there.
Yeah, yeah, exactly.
So I want to give them a response at least, but now I can do that and, you know, ease my guilty conscience without actually having to type out, you know, a hundred emails a month.
Yeah.
And then talk to me a little bit more about the, and this is great, by the way.
I mean, we, you know, we are obviously, yes, we're AI centric, but we are also automation centric, right?
Like we don't, we're very agnostic when.
When comes to tools, we'll use anything.
You know, I personally have used Zapier, obviously, over the years for various things.
But we have a, we just generally have a policy where people need to, like, clear their road for the things that are important.
Like, we didn't hire humans in the loop so that those humans could do repetitive tasks, right?
Of course.
It's just, like, a really ground-level thing.
And we do like people coming in with that mentality already, where it's like, hey, is this something I did twice this week?
Can I do it zero times next week?
You know what mean?
And I think that's kind of where we have, obviously, you, at a larger impact level, that's the tooling that we're building.
Of course.
However, there are an infinite number of ways that you can clear the road to actually, like, pay attention to the thing that matters.
That aren't just about art.
So we're very agnostic on tooling, whatever it takes to get the job done.
We often prototype things that end up in our pipelines using a combination of things like cloud projects or custom GPTs or automation tooling, off-the-shelf automation tooling.
I just prototyped something for a client in Lovable last week, and they were like, oh, this looks great.
I'm like, cool, now we'll go build that thing.
So it's just a very straightforward, whatever it takes to get the job done.
Please be proactive, figure it out.
So all of that stuff is great.
You won't have to regress in any way there.
I love hearing that.

Because once you've set up a workflow or an automation that takes care of a repetitive task, you don't want to have to go back to the drawing board and find another tool within the tech stack that you have to work with to rebuild that thing and reinvent the wheel.
So that's really lovely to hear.
It feels like a small thing in the broader scope of things.
But...
But it also is such a time suck and it forces you to switch contexts so much.
Yeah, it's the soul crushing stuff.
It's the stuff you hate to think about when you wake up in the morning instead of the thing you're like, oh, well, this is a really cool problem.
I'm so glad I get to work on this.
It's like, oh, now I got to do this junk.
You know what I mean?
It's like, ugh.
So if you can do that, if you're able to clear the road for that, generally speaking, people have more longevity at a job.
They feel more engaged.
And they do better work at the stuff that actually matters.

I don't care.

know what mean?

I just need to let it go.

Like that's what a lot of these products have been built for before.

Of what good quality is.

You also need to build validations in between each step.
LLMs as

What is it that they will find most valuable here?

really, it's about working from outcomes backwards.

How much ARR is a piece helping bring in, whether it's immediately after someone reads it, or further down the pipeline, maybe they read a guide, and then eventually they come to a webinar in two months, and they convert six months down the line, whatever it is.
